title: Luka Saxena
text:
Health Data Science · Research Assistant · UNSW MSc Candidate
Research Assistant at The George Institute for Global Health and MSc candidate in Health Data Science at UNSW. I work at the intersection of clinical research and data science – building the tools, analyses, and visualisations that move research forward.

title: Luka Saxena
text:
Health Data Science · Research Assistant · UNSW MSc Candidate
Research Assistant at The George Institute for Global Health and MSc candidate in Health Data Science at UNSW. I work at the intersection of clinical research and data science – building the tools, analyses, and visualisations that move research forward.

title: Luka Saxena
text:
Current Role
I’m a Research Assistant at The George Institute for Global Health, embedded in the Critical Care team. My primary project is SEPSIS SUPPORT – an NHMRC-funded program co-designing the first targeted models of post-sepsis care in Australia. Sepsis affects around 55,000 Australians each year, yet support after hospital discharge remains critically limited. The project brings together survivors, clinicians, and caregivers to change that.

title: Luka Saxena
text:
Goals & Aspirations


MSc Health Data Science
Building a rigorous foundation in statistical modelling, machine learning, and reproducible research through my Masters at UNSW – and writing about what I learn along the way.


Applying Skills in the Field
Taking what I learn directly into live research at The George Institute – developing the data infrastructure, analysis pipelines, and visualisations that drive SEPSIS SUPPORT forward.


Towards a PhD
Developing ideas at the intersection of health data science and critical care research, with a longer-term goal of pursuing a PhD that bridges both.

title: PRISMA2020 on R
text:
If you have ever put together a systematic review, you will know the PRISMA flow diagram: that box-and-arrow chart showing how many records you found, screened, excluded, and finally included. For a long time I built these by hand, nudging boxes around and re-typing numbers every time a count changed. It worked, but it was fiddly and very easy to get wrong.
Then I found the PRISMA2020 R package, and it changed how I do this completely. Here is how I got it working, including where to find the template and the actual code I used.


PRISMA2020 was built by Haddaway and colleagues to produce flow diagrams that follow the PRISMA 2020 standard. You fill in a simple CSV with your record counts, point R at it, and the package draws the whole diagram for you. You can output a static image, or an interactive HTML version where each box carries a tooltip or a hyperlink.



Install it from CRAN:
install.packages("PRISMA2020")
library(PRISMA2020)
To locate the bundled template and see where it lives on your machine:
csvFile <- system.file("extdata", "PRISMA.csv", package = "PRISMA2020")
csvFile   # prints the full path so you can open and copy it
Open that file, save a copy into your project, and fill in your own numbers.



The template is one row of data with a column for each number the diagram needs: records from databases and registers, records from other sources such as citation searching and websites, duplicates removed, records screened and excluded, reports sought and not retrieved, and so on.
For my review the records came in from seven different sources, so I made sure each one landed in the right column before reading the file back into R.



Once the CSV was filled in, the rest was short:
# Read in my filled-in template
data <- read.csv("data/prisma.csv")

# Convert it into the structure the package expects
data <- PRISMA_data(data)

# Build the diagram
plot <- PRISMA_flowdiagram(
  data,
  fontsize    = 12,
  interactive = FALSE,   # set TRUE for an interactive HTML version
  previous    = FALSE,   # I had no previous-version arm
  other       = TRUE     # I did have "other" sources, so keep this on
)

plot

PRISMA_save(plot, filename = "prisma_flowdiagram.png", filetype = "PNG")
Two arguments are worth knowing, because they caught me out at first. previous controls the left-hand arm for studies carried over from an earlier version of the review, and other controls the arm for records found through methods other than database searching. If your diagram looks off or has empty boxes, these two toggles are usually the culprit.



Here is the finished diagram for my review:

title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
Hi, I’m Luka. Thanks for stopping by. This is a small corner of the internet where I plan to think out loud about working with data. I’ve started it for a simple reason: I’m learning a lot right now, and I wanted somewhere to keep track of it all, share the good bits, and look back later on how far I’ve come.
By day I work in health and medical research, and on the side I’m studying health data science. Most of my hours go into wrangling messy data, picking up new tools, and trying to turn raw numbers into something clear and, on a good day, genuinely beautiful. This blog is where I’ll share that journey as it actually unfolds: the things I’m learning, the problems I get stuck on, and the small wins that make everything click into place.
A few things you can expect to find here:

Notes from my growth as a data scientist, and how I’m getting there
Fun data tips, tricks, and shortcuts I wish I’d known sooner
Visualisations and experiments, the prettier side of working with data
Honest reflections on finding my way in this field and building a career in it

I’m also writing this partly for myself. Think of it as a personal repository for the cool things I pick up along the way, the kind of stuff that’s too good to forget but too easily lost in a tangle of bookmarks and half-remembered tutorials. If it ends up helping you too, even better. So have a look around. A lot of this is still a work in progress, and that’s exactly the point. Come back now and then to see what’s new.
Thanks for reading,
Luka
